content.full_text:
RAy! MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
May 24,
1995
Jonathan P.
Lowell,
Esq.
City Attorney
City of San Bruno
567 El Caluino Real
San Bruno,
CA
94066
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-95—132
Dear Mr.
Lowell:
This is in response to your letter requesting assistance
concerning the responsibilities of City of San Bruno City
Councilmember Bill Baker pursuant to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 Your request
refers to decisions that may have already occurred.
We are unable
to provide you assistance with respect to past conduct.
(Regulation 18329.)
Therefore,
we are treating your letter as a
request for informal assistance with respect to prospective
decisions of the City of San Bruno.
2
Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any
conduct which may have already taken place.
In addition, this
letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The Commission does
not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
— 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—95—132
Page
2
QUESTION
May Vice—Mayor Baker participate in decisions of the city’s
CityNet subcommittee concerning the future acquisition of computer
technology for the city, despite the fact that he holds an
ownership interest in a business which develops computer hardware
and software and which operates a computer bulletin board system?
CONCLUSION
Vice-Mayor Baker may not participate in any decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on his
business, his sources of income, or any other economic interest of
his.
FACTS
Bill Baker is a member of the San Bruno City Council and
serves as the city’s vice—mayor.
He holds greater than a ten
percent interest in a partnership,
Main Street Company,
a business
which develops and markets computer hardware and software and
operates a computer bulletin board system
(a “BBS”).
The BBS is accessible to those persons who have a computer
and a computer modem.
The BBS is used to facilitate file
transfers to clients, provide online information about Mr.
Baker’s
company,
and gives users access- to other information and functions
such as conferences,
chat functions, etc.
In addition, it
provides information related to the City of San Bruno,
such as
city council agendas and other public records.
A limited part of
the BBS is provided to the general public free of charge as a
community service.
Other parts of the BBS are available only to
subscribers who pay a fee toaccess these parts.
-
—
The San Bruno City Council has formed a subcommittee,
known
as the CityNet subcommittee, to examine the possibility of
providing city information to those persons with computer and
computer modems.
This information would include agendas for
upcoming council meetings, minutes from private meetings, city
finances,
and historical information.
Mr.
Baker is a member of
the CityNet subcommittee.
On the basis of the vice-mayor’s letter
to you dated September 5,
1994, the vice-mayor’s role is to
provide the city council with general information concerning the
utilization of online computer systems to ultimately provide
greater public access to city information and services.
On the basis of other information provided with your request,
it appears that it is anticipated that the city may expend public
moneys on goods and/or technical services necessary to implement
the CityNet online computer system in an amount ranging from
$15,000 to $50,000.


Our File No.
1-95—132
Page
3
ANALYSIS
The Political Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
State of California by initiative in 1974.
The purpose for the
conflict—of—interest provisions of the Act was to ensure that
public officials, whether elected or appointed,
would perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by
their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).)
As a member
of the San Bruno City Council, Vice-Mayor Bill Baker is a “public
official” as defined in the Act.
(Section 82048.)
In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act further
provides that no public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt
to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official knows or has reason to know he or
she has a financial interest.
Participation in a Governmental Decision
Your letter states that Vice-Mayor Baker will abstain from
voting on any matter regarding CityNet that comes before the city
council.
However, the issue you present is whether it is
permissible for Vice-Mayor Baker to participate in the city’s
CityNet subcommittee to examine ways for making city government
information available through computer phone lines.
Although the
Act does not prevent the vice—mayor from serving in the
subcommittee, as noted above, pursuant to Section 87100,
he is
prohibited from making, participating in making,
or in any way
attempting to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he or she knows or has reason to
know
3 he has a financial interest.
(Regulations 18700 and
18700.1, copies enclosed.)
-
As a general rule,
a public official “makes a governmental
decision,” when the official, acting within the authority of his
or her office or position:
(1) votes on a matter;
(2)
appoints a
person;
(3) obligates or commits his or her agency to any course
of action; or
(4)
enters into any contractual agreement on behalf
of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18700(b).)
An official knows that he or she has a financial interest in a
decision if the official actually knows that it is reasonably
foreseeable that a decision will materially affect an economic
interest of the official.
For example,
as a general rule,
an
official “has reason to know” that a decision will affect a source
of income whenever a reasonable person, under the same
circumstances, would be likely to know the identity of the source
of income and would be aware of the decision’s probable impact on
the source.
(Vagim Advice Letter No.
1-89-688; Price Advice
Letter,
No. A-85-165.)


Our File No.
1-95-132
Page
4
A public official “participates in making a governmental
decision”
(Regulation 18700(c))
when, acting within the authority
of his or her position, the official:
(1)
Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with
a governmental entity or private person regarding a governmental
decision to:
(a)
Approve a rate, rule, or regulation;
(b)
Adopt or enforce a law;
(C)
Issue, deny,
suspend, or revoke any permit,
license, application, certificate, approval, order,
or similar authorization or entitlement;
(d)
Authorize the agency to enter into, modify,
or
renew a contract provided it is the type of
contract which requires agency approval;
(e)
Grant agency approval to a contract which requires
agency approval and in which the agency is a party
or to the specifications for such a contract;
(f)
Grant agency approval to a plan, design, report,
study,
or similar item; or
(g)
Adopt,
or grant agency approval of, policies,
standards,
or guidelines for the agency, or for any
subdivision thereof.
(2)
Advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
either directly or without significant intervening substantive
review
1 by conductinq research,
making any investigation,
or
preparing or presenting any report, analysis,
or opinion, orally,
or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part
of the official and the purpose of which is to influence a
governmental decision enumerated above in
(1) (a)
through (g).
Finally, with regard to a governmental decision which is
within or before an official’s agency or an agency appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of the agency, the official is
attempting to use the official’s position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before,
or otherwise attempts to
influence, any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.
Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client,
or customer.
(Regulation 18700.1(a).)
Based on the above, for example, if the vice—mayor had a
conflict of interest by virtue of an economic interest as
discussed below, not only would he be prohibited from voting, but


Our File No.
1-95-132
Page 5
he could not contact or make a recommendation to any official of
his agency to discuss the matter for any purpose.
Financial Interests
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision
4 if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
*
*
*
*
(C)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
Sections 87103(a),
(b),
and (d).
-
Vice-Mayor Baker holds greater than a ten percent interest iii
a business which develops and markets computer hardware and
software and which operates a computer bulletin board system.
The
public official’s business entity is a potentially disqualifying
economic interest of his within the meaning of Sections 87103 (a)
and (d).
In addition,
any person or business that has made any
payment to him of $250 or more in the past 12 months is a
Please note that while generally,
every governmental decision
must be analyzed independently to determine whether a conflict of
interest exists, under some circumstances a series of decisions
may be too interrelated to be considered separately.
(Miller
Advice Letter,
No. A-82-1l9.)
A “business entity” means any organization or enterprise
operated for profit,
including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership,
firm, business trust, joint venture,
syndicate, corporation or association.
(Section 82005.)


Our File No.
1-95-132
Page
6
potentially disqualifying source of income
6 of his for the
purposes of Section 87103(c).
Consequently, he may not
participate in any governmental decision if he knows or has reason
to know the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his business or a person or business entity
that has been a source of income to him of $250 or more in the
last 12 months.
Foreseeabi 1ity
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not recmired.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198,
copy enclosed.)
You have provided no facts concerning the potential impact of
the subcommittee’s decisions on the vice—mayor’s business or
clients of his business.
However, the general rule set forth in
In re Thorner (supra) is that where the business entity in which
the official has an economic interest makes a bid on a contract or
is preparing to make a bid,
a financial effect on the business
entity is reasonably foreseeably even if there is substantial
competition.
(In re Thorner, supra.)
In addition, this same rule
of foreseeability applies to decisions that lead up to the
contract,
such as the decision that sets the foundation for the
contractual relationship.
Thus, for example, if the CityNet
subcommittee made decisions (including recommendations)
concerning
a contract on which the vice—mayor’s business, or a source of
income of his, is planning to bid, it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decisions will have a financial effect-on the vi-ce--
-
mayor’s economic interests.
Materiality
However, in addition, the foreseeable effect on the vice—
mayor’s economic interests also must be material to require
disqualification.
The Commission has adopted regulations which
provide guidance on whether the reasonably foreseeable effects of
a decision will be considered material.
Regulation 18702
(copy
6
Income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any
income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or
spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially,
a 10-percent
interest or greater.
(Section 82030(a).)


Our File No.
1-95-132
Page
7
enclosed)
sets forth the guidelines for determining whether an
official’s economic interest in a decision is “materially”
affected as required by Section 87103.
If the official’s economic
interest is directly involved in the decision, then Regulation
18702.1
(copy enclosed)
applies to determine materiality.
Regulation 18702.1(b)
describes when a subject is deemed to be
directly involved in a decision.
On the other hand, if the
official’s economic interest is indirectly affected by the
decision, then Regulations 18702.2 to 18702.6
(copies enclosed)
apply to determine whether the effect of the decision is material.
Public Generally
A public official may still participate in governmental
decisions where he or she is disqualified where the material
financial effect of a governmental decision on the official’s
interests is indistinguishable from its effect on the public
generally as defined in Regulation 18703.
(Section 87103.)
However, the “public generally” exception does not appear
applicable to your facts.
I trust this letter has addressed your general concerns.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter or a
specific prospective decision that you would like advice on,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
General Counsel
A-)
-
By:
Luisa Mehchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
Enclosures

technology for the city, despite the fact that he holds an

and software and which operates a computer bulletin board system?

Vice-Mayor Baker may not participate in any decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on his
business, his sources of income, or any other economic interest of
his.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Vice—Mayor Baker participate in decisions of the city’s
CityNet subcommittee concerning the future acquisition of computer
technology for the city, despite the fact that he holds an
ownership interest in a business which develops computer hardware
and software and which operates a computer bulletin board system?

CONCLUSION: Vice-Mayor Baker may not participate in any decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on his
business, his sources of income, or any other economic interest of
his.
